name: Economics
description: A comprehensive introduction to micro and macroeconomics — scarcity, supply and demand, market structures, GDP, inflation, monetary and fiscal policy, international trade, and personal finance.

modules.title: Scarcity, Choice & Opportunity Cost
modules.titleZh: 稀缺性、选择与机会成本
modules.objectives: Explain the economic problem of scarcity; apply the concept of opportunity cost; interpret the production possibilities frontier.
modules.readingNote: Khan Academy — Scarcity and Economic Choices
modules.video2Note: Crash Course Economics #1 — Scarcity & the Invisible Hand
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Production Possibilities Curve practice
modules.assignment: Draw a production possibilities frontier for a country choosing between producing food and clothing. Identify two points (one efficient, one inefficient, one impossible) and explain what moving along the curve reveals about opportunity cost.

modules.title: Supply & Demand
modules.objectives: State the laws of supply and demand; identify factors that shift each curve; find equilibrium price and quantity; analyze price ceilings and floors.
modules.readingNote: Khan Academy — Supply and Demand article
modules.video2Note: Crash Course Economics #4 — Supply and Demand
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Supply & Demand equilibrium practice
modules.assignment: Choose a real product (e.g., electric vehicles, avocados). Identify one event that shifted supply and one that shifted demand in the past three years. Draw the resulting shifts and predict the new equilibrium.

modules.title: Elasticity
modules.objectives: Calculate price elasticity of demand and supply; distinguish elastic, inelastic, and unit-elastic goods; apply income and cross-price elasticity.
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Price Elasticity of Demand
modules.assignment: Select two goods: one you expect to be price-inelastic and one price-elastic. Calculate the elasticity coefficient for each using hypothetical data, and explain what factors account for the difference.

modules.title: Market Structures
modules.objectives: Compare perfect competition, monopoly, monopolistic competition, and oligopoly; analyze pricing power and efficiency in each structure.
modules.video2Note: Crash Course Economics #7 — Monopolies and Oligopolies
modules.assignment: Classify three real-world industries into different market structures. For each, justify your classification using characteristics such as number of firms, product differentiation, and barriers to entry.

modules.title: Market Failures & Government Intervention
modules.objectives: Define market failure; distinguish positive and negative externalities; explain public goods and information asymmetry; evaluate government policy responses.
modules.assignment: Identify one negative externality and one positive externality in your community. For each, propose a government policy intervention (tax, subsidy, regulation) and explain how it would move the market toward a socially optimal outcome.

modules.title: Measuring the Economy
modules.objectives: Explain how GDP is calculated; distinguish GDP from GNP; interpret CPI and the unemployment rate; identify limitations of economic indicators.
modules.video2Note: Crash Course Economics #7 — GDP & Measuring the Economy
modules.assignment: Look up the most recent GDP, CPI, and unemployment rate for any two countries. Compare these indicators and write a paragraph assessing which economy appears healthier and why.

modules.title: Economic Growth & Business Cycles
modules.objectives: Identify factors that drive long-run economic growth; describe phases of the business cycle; explain the role of leading economic indicators.
modules.assignment: Research a historical recession (e.g., 2008 financial crisis or 2020 COVID recession). Identify the phase, its causes, two indicators that predicted it, and how long recovery took.

modules.title: Money, Banking & the Federal Reserve
modules.objectives: List the three functions of money; explain how banks create money through fractional reserve banking; describe the Federal Reserve's role and monetary policy tools.
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — The Federal Reserve and Monetary Policy
modules.video2Note: Crash Course Economics #24 — Money and Finance
modules.assignment: Explain how a $1,000 deposit in a bank with a 10% reserve requirement can ultimately expand the money supply. Calculate the total potential money creation using the money multiplier formula.

modules.title: Inflation & Interest Rates
modules.objectives: Distinguish demand-pull and cost-push inflation; explain the Fisher effect; compare real and nominal interest rates; analyze how inflation affects borrowers, lenders, and the economy.
modules.video2Note: Crash Course Economics #35 — Inflation and Bubbles
modules.assignment: If the nominal interest rate is 6% and inflation is 4%, what is the real interest rate? Explain who benefits and who is hurt when actual inflation turns out to be higher than expected.

modules.title: Fiscal Policy
modules.objectives: Distinguish expansionary and contractionary fiscal policy; evaluate effects of government spending and taxation; compare Keynesian and classical perspectives on fiscal stimulus.
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Fiscal Policy and Stimulus
modules.assignment: Suppose the economy is in a recession with 9% unemployment. Write a short policy memo recommending either a Keynesian or classical response. Specify the tools (tax cuts, spending increases, etc.) and anticipate one criticism of your approach.

modules.title: International Trade & Globalization
modules.objectives: Apply comparative advantage to explain patterns of trade; evaluate the effects of tariffs and quotas; assess the role of the WTO; interpret the balance of payments.
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Comparative Advantage and Gains from Trade
modules.assignment: Using a numerical example with two countries and two goods, demonstrate comparative advantage and show how trade leaves both countries better off. Then argue for or against a specific tariff currently in the news.

modules.title: Exchange Rates & International Finance
modules.objectives: Explain how exchange rates are determined; distinguish currency appreciation and depreciation; analyze how exchange rate changes affect exports, imports, and the trade balance.
modules.assignment: If the US dollar appreciates against the Chinese yuan, explain the effect on (a) US exports to China, (b) US imports from China, and (c) the US trade deficit. Use a supply-and-demand diagram for currencies to illustrate.

modules.title: Income Distribution & Poverty
modules.objectives: Interpret the Lorenz curve and Gini coefficient; identify causes of income inequality; evaluate anti-poverty policies including transfer payments and minimum wage.
modules.readingNote: Khan Academy — Poverty and Income Distribution article
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Lorenz Curve and Gini Coefficient
modules.video2Note: Crash Course Economics #17 — Income and Wealth Inequality
modules.assignment: Look up the Gini coefficient for three countries. Rank them by inequality and explain two structural factors (e.g., education access, tax policy) that might account for the differences.

modules.title: Personal Finance & Behavioral Economics
modules.titleZh: 个人理财与行为经济学
modules.objectives: Apply budgeting and saving principles; explain compound interest; identify common behavioral biases that affect financial decisions; evaluate credit and investment options.
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — The Importance of Saving
modules.assignment: Create a monthly budget for a hypothetical person earning $2,500 per month. Allocate for housing, food, transportation, savings (at least 10%), and discretionary spending. Calculate how much they would accumulate in 10 years if they invested their savings at 6% annual return.

questions.question: Which of the following best illustrates the concept of scarcity?
options: A country with unlimited natural resources
A student who must choose between studying and working due to limited time
A government that can print unlimited money
questions.answer: A student who must choose between studying and working due to limited time

questions.question: A point inside (below) the production possibilities frontier indicates:
questions.answer: Productive inefficiency

questions.question: If the price of a good rises and, as a result, the quantity demanded falls, this best illustrates:
options: A shift of the demand curve
questions.answer: The law of demand

questions.question: A price ceiling set below the equilibrium price causes:
options: An increase in the equilibrium price
questions.answer: A shortage

questions.question: If a 10% price increase leads to a 25% decrease in quantity demanded, the good is:
questions.answer: Price elastic

questions.question: Which market structure has the most firms selling identical products with no barriers to entry?
questions.answer: Perfect competition

questions.question: A negative externality occurs when:
options: Production benefits third parties not involved in the transaction
Production costs to society exceed private costs
questions.answer: Production costs to society exceed private costs

questions.question: GDP is best defined as:
options: Total income earned by a country's citizens worldwide
The total market value of all final goods and services produced within a country in a given year
The total value of all transactions in an economy
The sum of all wages paid to workers
questions.answer: The total market value of all final goods and services produced within a country in a given year

questions.question: The unemployment rate measures the percentage of:
options: People in the labor force who are actively seeking work but cannot find it
All adults who are not employed
People working part-time who want full-time work
questions.answer: People in the labor force who are actively seeking work but cannot find it

questions.question: During the expansion phase of the business cycle:
questions.answer: GDP rises and unemployment falls

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT one of the three functions of money?
questions.answer: Source of government revenue

questions.question: If the reserve requirement is 20%, the money multiplier is:
Demand-pull inflation is caused by:
options: Supply shortages due to natural disasters
Excess aggregate demand exceeding productive capacity
questions.answer: Excess aggregate demand exceeding productive capacity

questions.question: Explain the concept of opportunity cost using a real-life example. Why must every economic choice involve a trade-off?
questions.answer: Opportunity cost is the value of the next-best alternative forgone when a choice is made. For example, if a student uses Saturday to work a part-time job, the opportunity cost is the studying or leisure time given up. Every choice involves a trade-off because resources (time, money, land) are scarce — using them one way means they cannot simultaneously be used another way.

questions.question: Describe two ways the government can correct a negative externality such as air pollution. Evaluate one advantage and one limitation of each method.
questions.answer: The government can use (1) a Pigouvian tax (pollution tax) that raises the private cost to equal the social cost, reducing output to the socially optimal level — advantage: creates revenue and price signal; limitation: difficult to set the correct tax rate. (2) Regulation (emission standards) directly limits pollution — advantage: provides a clear legal ceiling; limitation: less flexible and can be costly to enforce.

questions.question: The law of supply states that, all else equal, as price rises:
questions.answer: Quantity supplied rises

questions.question: If consumer incomes rise and the demand for a good increases, that good is classified as:
questions.answer: A normal good

questions.question: Cross-price elasticity of demand measures how:
options: A price change in one good affects demand for another good
Price changes affect the quantity supplied
Quantity demanded responds to its own price
questions.answer: A price change in one good affects demand for another good

questions.question: A monopolist maximizes profit by producing where:
questions.answer: Marginal revenue equals marginal cost

questions.question: A public good is defined as one that is:
questions.answer: Non-excludable and non-rival in consumption

questions.question: Real GDP differs from nominal GDP in that real GDP:
options: Is adjusted for price level changes (inflation)
questions.answer: Is adjusted for price level changes (inflation)

questions.question: Which of the following best describes a leading economic indicator?
options: A variable that changes after the economy as a whole changes
A variable that tends to change before the economy as a whole changes
A measure of past GDP growth
questions.answer: A variable that tends to change before the economy as a whole changes

questions.question: When the Federal Reserve raises the federal funds rate, the likely effect is:
options: Lower borrowing costs and increased spending
Higher borrowing costs and reduced inflation
A weaker dollar and more exports
questions.answer: Higher borrowing costs and reduced inflation

questions.question: The Fisher equation states that the nominal interest rate approximately equals:
options: Inflation minus the real interest rate
The real interest rate plus expected inflation
The federal funds rate divided by the CPI
questions.answer: The real interest rate plus expected inflation

questions.question: Expansionary fiscal policy during a recession involves:
options: Raising taxes and cutting government spending
Increasing government spending and/or cutting taxes
The central bank lowering interest rates
questions.answer: Increasing government spending and/or cutting taxes

questions.question: Comparative advantage means a country should specialize in producing goods where it has:
options: The lowest opportunity cost relative to trading partners
questions.answer: The lowest opportunity cost relative to trading partners

questions.question: When the U.S. dollar depreciates against foreign currencies:
options: U.S. exports become more expensive for foreign buyers
U.S. imports become cheaper for Americans
U.S. exports become cheaper for foreign buyers
The U.S. trade deficit automatically disappears
questions.answer: U.S. exports become cheaper for foreign buyers

questions.question: A Gini coefficient of 0 represents:
questions.answer: Perfect income equality

questions.question: Compound interest differs from simple interest because compound interest:
options: Applies only to the original principal each period
Earns interest on both the principal and previously accumulated interest
Is only offered by the government
questions.answer: Earns interest on both the principal and previously accumulated interest

questions.question: The 'anchoring' bias in behavioral economics refers to:
options: Overweighting recent events when making decisions
Relying too heavily on the first piece of information encountered
questions.answer: Relying too heavily on the first piece of information encountered

questions.question: A tariff on imported steel most directly:
options: Lowers the domestic price of steel
Raises the domestic price of steel and protects domestic producers
Increases the quantity of steel imported
questions.answer: Raises the domestic price of steel and protects domestic producers

questions.question: Structural unemployment occurs when:
options: Workers are between jobs during normal labor market transitions
Workers' skills do not match the skills demanded by employers
The economy is in a recession and firms lay off workers
Workers choose not to work at the going wage
questions.answer: Workers' skills do not match the skills demanded by employers

questions.question: Using the concept of comparative advantage, explain why two countries can both benefit from trade even if one country is more efficient at producing everything. Provide a numerical example.
questions.answer: Comparative advantage holds that a country should specialize in producing the good for which it has the lowest opportunity cost. For example, if Country A can produce 10 cars or 20 tons of wheat per worker, and Country B can produce 2 cars or 8 tons of wheat per worker, Country A has a lower opportunity cost for cars (2 tons of wheat) while Country B has a lower opportunity cost for wheat (0.25 cars). By specializing and trading, both can consume beyond their individual production possibility frontiers, making both better off even though Country A is absolutely more productive in both goods.

questions.question: Explain the relationship between the money supply, inflation, and the Federal Reserve's tools for controlling inflation. What are the trade-offs a central bank faces when fighting inflation?
questions.answer: When the money supply grows faster than real output, too much money chases too few goods, causing inflation. The Federal Reserve can combat inflation by raising the federal funds rate (making borrowing more expensive), selling government securities (open market operations, which reduce bank reserves), or increasing reserve requirements. The trade-off is that these contractionary policies slow economic growth and raise unemployment — tightening too aggressively can push the economy into recession, while tightening too little allows inflation to persist and erode purchasing power.

questions.question: Define the Lorenz curve and the Gini coefficient. Explain two specific government policies that could reduce income inequality and one potential drawback of each.
questions.answer: The Lorenz curve plots the cumulative share of income received by the bottom X% of the population; perfect equality is a 45-degree diagonal line. The Gini coefficient measures the area between the Lorenz curve and the line of equality, ranging from 0 (perfect equality) to 1 (maximum inequality). Two policies: (1) Progressive income tax — higher earners pay higher rates, redistributing income; drawback: may reduce incentives for high earners to work or invest. (2) Expanded access to education and job training — raises earning potential for lower-income groups; drawback: benefits take years to materialize and require sustained funding.

quizQuestions.question: Scarcity in economics means that:
options: Only poor countries face resource limits
Resources are limited relative to unlimited wants
Goods are unavailable in the market
quizQuestions.answer: Resources are limited relative to unlimited wants

quizQuestions.question: The opportunity cost of a decision is:
options: The monetary price paid for a good
The total cost of all alternatives
The sunk cost of previous spending
quizQuestions.answer: The next-best alternative forgone

quizQuestions.question: A point outside the production possibilities frontier is:
options: Currently unattainable given existing resources and technology
quizQuestions.answer: Currently unattainable given existing resources and technology

quizQuestions.question: Trade-offs exist because:
options: Resources are scarce and have alternative uses
quizQuestions.answer: Resources are scarce and have alternative uses

quizQuestions.question: Economic growth is represented on a PPF diagram by:
options: A point moving inside the curve
A point moving along the curve
quizQuestions.answer: The curve shifting outward

quizQuestions.question: According to the law of demand, when the price of a good falls:
quizQuestions.answer: Quantity demanded rises

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following would shift the demand curve for coffee to the right?
options: An increase in the price of coffee
A rise in consumer incomes (if coffee is a normal good)
A decrease in the price of tea (a substitute)
quizQuestions.answer: A rise in consumer incomes (if coffee is a normal good)

quizQuestions.question: Market equilibrium occurs where:
options: Price is set by the government
quizQuestions.answer: Quantity supplied equals quantity demanded

quizQuestions.question: A price floor set above equilibrium results in:
quizQuestions.answer: A surplus

quizQuestions.question: If the supply of smartphones decreases while demand stays the same, the equilibrium price will:
quizQuestions.answer: Rise

quizQuestions.question: Price elasticity of demand measures:
options: How supply responds to price changes
How sensitive quantity demanded is to price changes
The slope of the supply curve
The change in income relative to price
quizQuestions.answer: How sensitive quantity demanded is to price changes

quizQuestions.question: Goods with close substitutes tend to have:
quizQuestions.answer: Elastic demand

quizQuestions.question: If a 5% rise in income leads to a 10% increase in demand for a good, the income elasticity is:
A positive cross-price elasticity between two goods indicates they are:
quizQuestions.answer: Substitutes

quizQuestions.question: When demand is perfectly inelastic, a price increase leads to:
options: A proportional decrease in quantity demanded
A large drop in quantity demanded
quizQuestions.answer: No change in quantity demanded

quizQuestions.question: In perfect competition, individual firms are price:
quizQuestions.answer: Takers

quizQuestions.question: A monopoly exists when:
options: Two large firms dominate a market
There are many firms selling differentiated products
A single firm is the sole seller with no close substitutes
quizQuestions.answer: A single firm is the sole seller with no close substitutes

quizQuestions.question: Monopolistic competition differs from perfect competition because firms in monopolistic competition:
options: Sell differentiated products and have some pricing power
Have no barriers to entry or exit
quizQuestions.answer: Sell differentiated products and have some pricing power

quizQuestions.question: An oligopoly is characterized by:
options: A few large, mutually interdependent firms
quizQuestions.answer: A few large, mutually interdependent firms

quizQuestions.question: Barriers to entry in a monopoly include all EXCEPT:
options: Economies of scale creating a natural monopoly
quizQuestions.answer: Large number of competing firms

quizQuestions.question: A negative externality results in market output that is:
quizQuestions.answer: Above the socially optimal level

quizQuestions.question: A positive externality occurs when:
options: A firm's production harms third parties
A transaction benefits third parties not involved in the exchange
Prices rise due to increased demand
quizQuestions.answer: A transaction benefits third parties not involved in the exchange

quizQuestions.question: National defense is a classic example of a public good because it is:
quizQuestions.answer: Non-excludable and non-rival

quizQuestions.question: Information asymmetry occurs when:
options: Both buyers and sellers have equal information
One party in a transaction has more relevant information than the other
The government controls all market information
quizQuestions.answer: One party in a transaction has more relevant information than the other

quizQuestions.question: A Pigouvian tax is designed to:
options: Correct a negative externality by making producers pay the social cost
quizQuestions.answer: Correct a negative externality by making producers pay the social cost

quizQuestions.question: Which expenditure category is NOT included in the GDP expenditure approach?
quizQuestions.answer: Transfer payments such as welfare

quizQuestions.question: The Consumer Price Index (CPI) measures:
options: Changes in the price level of a basket of goods and services over time
quizQuestions.answer: Changes in the price level of a basket of goods and services over time

quizQuestions.question: GNP differs from GDP in that GNP includes:
options: Production by a country's residents regardless of location, not foreigners within borders
quizQuestions.answer: Production by a country's residents regardless of location, not foreigners within borders

quizQuestions.question: A limitation of GDP as a measure of well-being is that it:
options: Does not account for income distribution, environmental quality, or leisure
Counts all transactions including illegal ones
quizQuestions.answer: Does not account for income distribution, environmental quality, or leisure

quizQuestions.question: Frictional unemployment refers to:
options: Workers temporarily between jobs due to normal labor market transitions
Workers whose skills no longer match available jobs
Workers who have given up looking for work
quizQuestions.answer: Workers temporarily between jobs due to normal labor market transitions

quizQuestions.question: The four phases of the business cycle in order are:
quizQuestions.answer: Expansion, peak, recession, trough

quizQuestions.question: Long-run economic growth is primarily driven by:
options: Improvements in technology and increases in capital and labor
quizQuestions.answer: Improvements in technology and increases in capital and labor

quizQuestions.question: A recession is technically defined as:
options: One quarter of negative GDP growth
Two consecutive quarters of negative GDP growth
A fall in stock market prices
quizQuestions.answer: Two consecutive quarters of negative GDP growth

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following is a leading economic indicator?
quizQuestions.answer: New housing permits

quizQuestions.question: Human capital refers to:
options: The stock of knowledge, skills, and health of workers
Financial investments in stocks and bonds
quizQuestions.answer: The stock of knowledge, skills, and health of workers

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following best describes M1 money supply?
options: Currency in circulation plus checking deposits
The value of all financial assets
quizQuestions.answer: Currency in circulation plus checking deposits

quizQuestions.question: Fractional reserve banking means banks:
options: Lend out a portion of deposits while keeping a fraction in reserve
quizQuestions.answer: Lend out a portion of deposits while keeping a fraction in reserve

quizQuestions.question: The Federal Reserve's primary tool for conducting monetary policy is:
options: Open market operations (buying and selling government securities)
quizQuestions.answer: Open market operations (buying and selling government securities)

quizQuestions.question: When the Federal Reserve buys government bonds, the money supply:
quizQuestions.answer: Expands

quizQuestions.question: The discount rate is the interest rate the Federal Reserve charges:
quizQuestions.answer: Commercial banks for borrowing reserves

quizQuestions.question: Inflation is defined as:
options: A one-time increase in a single good's price
A sustained increase in the overall price level
A decrease in the money supply
quizQuestions.answer: A sustained increase in the overall price level

quizQuestions.question: Cost-push inflation is caused by:
options: Increases in production costs such as rising oil prices
quizQuestions.answer: Increases in production costs such as rising oil prices

quizQuestions.question: If the nominal interest rate is 8% and inflation is 3%, the real interest rate is:
Inflation generally hurts:
options: People who hold large amounts of cash savings
quizQuestions.answer: People who hold large amounts of cash savings

quizQuestions.question: The Fisher effect states that a rise in expected inflation leads to:
options: A fall in nominal interest rates
A rise in nominal interest rates by approximately the same amount
A fall in real interest rates
quizQuestions.answer: A rise in nominal interest rates by approximately the same amount

quizQuestions.question: Fiscal policy refers to government decisions about:
options: Interest rates and the money supply
quizQuestions.answer: Taxation and government spending

quizQuestions.question: A budget deficit occurs when:
options: The national debt is paid off
quizQuestions.answer: Government spending exceeds tax revenue

quizQuestions.question: According to Keynesian economics, during a recession the government should:
options: Increase spending to stimulate aggregate demand
quizQuestions.answer: Increase spending to stimulate aggregate demand

quizQuestions.question: The fiscal multiplier effect suggests that:
options: Government spending increases GDP by less than the spending itself
A dollar of government spending can increase GDP by more than a dollar
Tax cuts always reduce economic output
Government debt has no effect on the economy
quizQuestions.answer: A dollar of government spending can increase GDP by more than a dollar

quizQuestions.question: Classical economists argue that markets are best left alone because:
options: Prices and wages adjust automatically to restore full employment
Recessions never occur in free markets
quizQuestions.answer: Prices and wages adjust automatically to restore full employment

quizQuestions.question: A country has a comparative advantage in a good when it can produce it at:
options: Higher absolute output than any other country
A lower opportunity cost than other countries
quizQuestions.answer: A lower opportunity cost than other countries

quizQuestions.question: A tariff is best defined as:
options: A limit on the quantity of imports allowed
A subsidy given to domestic exporters
quizQuestions.answer: A tax on imported goods

quizQuestions.question: The World Trade Organization (WTO) was established to:
options: Promote free trade by reducing barriers and resolving disputes
Provide development aid to poor countries
quizQuestions.answer: Promote free trade by reducing barriers and resolving disputes

quizQuestions.question: The current account of the balance of payments records:
options: Trade in goods and services plus income and transfers
Changes in a country's foreign exchange reserves
quizQuestions.answer: Trade in goods and services plus income and transfers

quizQuestions.question: Free trade is generally supported by economists because it:
options: Guarantees full employment in all sectors
Allows countries to specialize and increases total output and consumption
Always benefits every worker in every country
quizQuestions.answer: Allows countries to specialize and increases total output and consumption

quizQuestions.question: If the dollar buys more euros than before, the dollar has:
quizQuestions.answer: Appreciated against the euro

quizQuestions.question: A weaker domestic currency tends to:
options: Make exports more expensive for foreigners
Make imports cheaper for domestic consumers
quizQuestions.answer: Make exports cheaper for foreigners

quizQuestions.question: Purchasing power parity (PPP) theory suggests that in the long run:
options: Exchange rates are set by governments
Exchange rates adjust so that identical goods cost the same in different countries
Wealthier countries always have stronger currencies
Interest rates determine all exchange rate movements
quizQuestions.answer: Exchange rates adjust so that identical goods cost the same in different countries

quizQuestions.question: A trade deficit occurs when:
options: The government spends more than it collects in taxes
quizQuestions.answer: Imports exceed exports

quizQuestions.question: Foreign direct investment (FDI) refers to:
options: A company establishing or acquiring business operations in another country
quizQuestions.answer: A company establishing or acquiring business operations in another country

quizQuestions.question: The Lorenz curve that bows further from the line of perfect equality indicates:
quizQuestions.answer: More income inequality

quizQuestions.question: A Gini coefficient closer to 1 indicates:
quizQuestions.answer: Extreme income inequality

quizQuestions.question: Structural causes of income inequality include:
options: Differences in education, skills, and discrimination
quizQuestions.answer: Differences in education, skills, and discrimination

quizQuestions.question: A progressive tax system means:
options: Everyone pays the same dollar amount
Everyone pays the same percentage of income
Higher-income earners pay a higher percentage of income in taxes
quizQuestions.answer: Higher-income earners pay a higher percentage of income in taxes

quizQuestions.question: The poverty line is typically defined as:
options: Earning less than the national average income
A minimum income threshold below which individuals cannot meet basic needs
Any household with more than three members
quizQuestions.answer: A minimum income threshold below which individuals cannot meet basic needs

quizQuestions.question: The 50/30/20 budgeting rule allocates income as:
quizQuestions.answer: 50% needs, 30% wants, 20% savings

quizQuestions.question: Compound interest grows wealth faster than simple interest because:
options: It applies a higher interest rate
Interest is earned on both the principal and accumulated interest
Banks charge lower fees for compound accounts
It is only available to wealthy investors
quizQuestions.answer: Interest is earned on both the principal and accumulated interest

quizQuestions.question: A credit score primarily reflects:
options: Your history of repaying debts on time
The size of your investment portfolio
The country you were born in
quizQuestions.answer: Your history of repaying debts on time

quizQuestions.question: The 'present bias' in behavioral economics refers to:
options: Overvaluing future rewards relative to immediate ones
Preferring immediate rewards over future ones, even when waiting is more rational
Anchoring decisions to the first price seen
Following the investment decisions of others
quizQuestions.answer: Preferring immediate rewards over future ones, even when waiting is more rational

quizQuestions.question: Diversification in investing means:
options: Putting all savings into one high-return asset
Spreading investments across different assets to reduce risk
quizQuestions.answer: Spreading investments across different assets to reduce risk
